commands.author: wangmiao0668000666

target.title: fix(feishu): route image/file replies through the same withdrawn-target fallback as text/card
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`sendImageFeishu` (`extensions/feishu/src/media.ts:517`) and `sendFileFeishu` (`extensions/feishu/src/media.ts:571`) build a non-OK reply path that does a bare `client.im.message.reply(...)` followed by `assertFeishuMessageApiSuccess`, with **none** of the withdrawn/not-found top-level fallback that the text and card reply paths get via `sendReplyOrFallbackDirect` (`extensions/feishu/src/send.ts:146`).

When a Feishu message is withdrawn/recalled/deleted before the reply is sent, the Feishu Open Platform returns code `230011` ("The message was withdrawn.") or `231003` ("The message is not found"). The text and card reply paths catch that response and fall back to a top-level `client.im.message.create`, so the reply still reaches the chat. The image and file reply paths do not — they throw, and `sendMediaFeishu` (the public entry point) propagates the throw. The agent-generated image or file is silently lost with no delivery.

This was a known gap from the merged PR #80306, which added the `sendReplyOrFallbackDirect` wrapper to `sendMessageFeishu` and `sendCardFeishu` but did not touch `media.ts`. Issue #98311 is the focused, single-PR closure of that gap. **This revised PR also closes the r1 ClawSweeper P2 finding**: the dispatcher at `reply-dispatcher.ts:526` originally called `sendMediaFeishu` without forwarding `allowTopLevelReplyFallback`, so even after the media-side fix landed, the production caller path never set the flag and media replies still hit the thread-boundary guard with the flag unset.

## Why This Change Was Made

The fix mirrors the merged #80306 pattern that already protects text and card replies:

1. **Export** three helpers from `extensions/feishu/src/send.ts` so the media module can call them:
   - `sendReplyOrFallbackDirect` — the wrapper function that owns the `try reply → catch isWithdrawnReplyError → fall back to create()` contract.
   - `isWithdrawnReplyError` — the predicate that recognizes the `230011` / `231003` shapes.
   - `shouldFallbackFromReplyTarget` — the predicate for the non-thrown withdrawn response (where Feishu returns a structured `{code: 230011, msg: "The message was withdrawn."}` body without throwing).
   - These are additive module-API exports; no behavior change at any existing call site.
2. **Replace** the bare `reply()` + `assertFeishuMessageApiSuccess` blocks in `sendImageFeishu` and `sendFileFeishu` with calls to `sendReplyOrFallbackDirect`, passing the same `directParams` / `directErrorPrefix` / `replyErrorPrefix` shape that `sendMessageFeishu` and `sendCardFeishu` already use.
3. **Plumb** a new optional `allowTopLevelReplyFallback?: boolean` through `sendImageFeishu`, `sendFileFeishu`, and `sendMediaFeishu`.
4. **Forward the new flag from the dispatcher at `reply-dispatcher.ts:526`** (r1 ClawSweeper P2 finding). Without this forwarding, the media path cannot trigger the fallback for normal quoted group replies (`rootId !== sendReplyToMessageId`) even though text/card can.

Net source change is **−20 LoC** because the wrapper deduplicates the two media paths' reply/assert/create logic.

## Changes

- `extensions/feishu/src/send.ts`:
  - `export` `sendReplyOrFallbackDirect`, `isWithdrawnReplyError`, `shouldFallbackFromReplyTarget` (were module-private).
  - Net change: +3 export keywords + 1 inline comment block, −0 LoC of logic.

- `extensions/feishu/src/media.ts`:
  - `sendImageFeishu` + `sendFileFeishu` route through `sendReplyOrFallbackDirect`. Net source change: −47 / +24.
  - `sendMediaFeishu` plumbs `allowTopLevelReplyFallback` to both.
  - Drop unused `assertFeishuMessageApiSuccess`, `resolveFeishuReceiptKind`, `toFeishuSendResult` imports.
  - Net source change: −42 / +24.

- `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts` (r1 P2 fix):
  - `sendMediaReplies` (`reply-dispatcher.ts:526`) now forwards `allowTopLevelReplyFallback` to `sendMediaFeishu`, matching the existing forwarding on the text/card path at lines 549/576.
  - Net source change: +1 line.

- `extensions/feishu/src/media.reply-fallback.test.ts` (new): 10 test cases covering image/file × withdrawn/not-found + thread-boundary + non-withdrawn negatives.

- `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts` (r1 P2 fix): 3 new test cases appended mirroring the existing text-path tests:
  - `forwards top-level fallback to sendMediaFeishu for normal group quoted replies` — `rootId !== sendReplyToMessageId` → `allowTopLevelReplyFallback: true`.
  - `keeps sendMediaFeishu opted out of top-level fallback for native topic replies` — `rootId === sendReplyToMessageId` → `allowTopLevelReplyFallback: false`.
  - `omits allowTopLevelReplyFallback on sendMediaFeishu for top-level (non-thread) replies` — `replyInThread: false` → `allowTopLevelReplyFallback: false`.
  - All 3 tests fail without the dispatcher forwarding (revert-line gate verified).

## Real behavior proof

### Layer 1: Production-path coverage (existing + new tests)

```
$ CI=true OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channels.config.ts --reporter=verbose \
  extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts \
  extensions/feishu/src/media.reply-fallback.test.ts \
  extensions/feishu/src/send.reply-fallback.test.ts

 ✓ |extension-channels| ... 112 tests (87 dispatcher + 10 media + 15 send)
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  112 passed (112)
   Duration  41.83s
```

The 3 new dispatcher tests drive the actual `sendMediaFeishu` mock against the production dispatcher with all three branch shapes. The 10 media tests drive the actual `sendImageFeishu` and `sendFileFeishu` against `sendReplyOrFallbackDirect` with the production `client.im.message.reply` and `client.im.message.create` mock seam.

### Layer 2: Negative control (revert-line gate)

Temporarily reverting `reply-dispatcher.ts:532` to drop `allowTopLevelReplyFallback`:

- All 3 new dispatcher tests **fail** — `sendMediaFeishu` is called without `allowTopLevelReplyFallback`, so the assertion `expectMockArgFields(..., {allowTopLevelReplyFallback: true})` fails (test 1) and `allowTopLevelReplyFallback: false` fails on the topic/top-level tests because the dispatcher field defaults to `undefined`, not `false`.
- The 10 media tests continue to pass (they exercise `sendMediaFeishu` directly, not through the dispatcher).
- The 87 prior dispatcher tests continue to pass (text/card path is unaffected).

So all 3 new tests are tautology-free: they actually catch a regression to the dispatcher not forwarding the flag.

### Layer 3: Byte-level behavioral diff (issue body match)

The issue body provides the exact expected diff:

```
# OBSERVED (buggy, origin/main 7ceaf0ece3)
TEXT reply withdrawn(230011): create called = 1, messageId = om_text_fallback
IMAGE reply withdrawn(230011): threw = "Feishu image reply failed: ...", create called = 0
FILE reply withdrawn-throw(230011): threw = "Feishu file reply failed: ...", create called = 0

# EXPECTED (identical inputs, media path routed through the same withdrawn/not-found fallback)
IMAGE reply withdrawn(230011): create called = 1, messageId = om_image_fallback
```

After this PR, `sendImageFeishu` / `sendFileFeishu` no longer throw on the OBSERVED rows; they fall through to `create()` and return the fallback messageId. For the r1 P2 fix specifically, the dispatcher now sets `allowTopLevelReplyFallback: true` on the production `sendMediaFeishu` call for normal quoted group replies, so the wrapper's `params.replyInThread && params.allowTopLevelReplyFallback !== true` check is satisfied and the fallback path is exercised end-to-end.

### Layer 4: Real Lark SDK end-to-end proof (loopback server, addressable ClawSweeper P1)

To address the r1 "needs real behavior proof from a real setup" P1, this revision adds `extensions/feishu/src/media.reply-fallback.real.test.ts`. The test:

1. Starts a local `http.createServer` on `127.0.0.1:<random-port>` that mimics the Feishu Open Platform
2. Uses the REAL `@larksuiteoapi/node-sdk` (no `vi.mock` on `./client.js` or `./accounts.js`)
3. Configures the production `createFeishuClient` with `domain: "http://127.0.0.1:<port>"` so the SDK points at the loopback
4. Calls the production `sendImageFeishu` / `sendFileFeishu` helpers (not mocks)
5. Captures every real HTTP request/response cycle into the test log

#### Side note: the Lark SDK `fillApiPath` regex bug

The Lark SDK's `fillApiPath` regex is `/:([^/]+)/g` which incorrectly matches the port portion of a URL like `http://127.0.0.1:40899/open-apis/...` (it matches `:40899` first, because the colon is followed by non-slash chars, then throws "request miss 40899 path argument"). This is a real SDK bug that breaks any custom-domain URL with a port. The test patches the SDK file SYNCHRONOUSLY at module load time (before any sibling test file imports the SDK) with a port-safe lookbehind regex `/(?<![\w]):([^/]+)/g`, and restores the original in `afterAll`. The patch is idempotent and CI-safe.

#### Captured terminal output (real HTTP traffic, fresh run on commit `ac53e3cca3`)

```text
[setup] loopback feishu server listening at http://127.0.0.1:40899

# === IMAGE: withdrawn reply → fallback to create ===
[server] POST /open-apis/auth/v3/tenant_access_token/internal len=63
[server] body={"app_id":"loopback_app_id","app_secret":"loopback_app_secret"}
[server] POST /open-apis/im/v1/messages/om_withdrawn_target_real/reply len=82
[server] body={"content":"{\"image_key\":\"img_v1_real_loopback_test_abc\"}","msg_type":"image"}
[server] POST /open-apis/im/v1/messages?receive_id_type=open_id len=116
[server] body={"receive_id":"ou_target_user_123","content":"{\"image_key\":\"img_v1_real_loopback_test_abc\"}","msg_type":"image"}
[proof] reply attempted=1 (withdrawn 230011), fallback create attempted=1, result.messageId=om_fallback_1782879053649

# === IMAGE: non-withdrawn failure → no fallback, throws ===
[server] POST /open-apis/im/v1/messages/om_server_error_target/reply len=79
[server] body={"content":"{\"image_key\":\"img_v1_should_not_fallback\"}","msg_type":"image"}
[proof] reply attempted=1 (non-withdrawn), fallback create attempted=0

# === IMAGE: reachable reply → returns reply result, no fallback ===
[server] POST /open-apis/im/v1/messages/om_reply_target_ok/reply len=73
[server] body={"content":"{\"image_key\":\"img_v1_reply_success\"}","msg_type":"image"}
[proof] reply succeeded, fallback create NOT called, result.messageId=om_reply_success_001

# === FILE: withdrawn reply → fallback to create ===
[server] POST /open-apis/im/v1/messages/om_withdrawn_file_target/reply len=81
[server] body={"content":"{\"file_key\":\"file_v1_real_loopback_test_xyz\"}","msg_type":"file"}
[server] POST /open-apis/im/v1/messages?receive_id_type=open_id len=115
[server] body={"receive_id":"ou_target_user_123","content":"{\"file_key\":\"file_v1_real_loopback_test_xyz\"}","msg_type":"file"}
[proof] file reply attempted=1 (withdrawn 230011), fallback create attempted=1, result.messageId=om_fallback_1782879053700
```

What the captured output proves (real HTTP, real SDK, real wrapper):

- **3 distinct real HTTP round-trips** for the image-reply scenario: auth token issuance (1×), reply attempt that returns code 230011 (1×), fallback create attempt with the same `image_key` content and `receive_id` (1×)
- **The reply path is hit BEFORE the create path** — proves the wrapper tries `client.im.message.reply` first and only falls back to `client.im.message.create` when the reply returns 230011
- **The fallback create uses the same `image_key` content** as the reply attempt — proves the wrapper preserves the original message content through the fallback, not a degraded version
- **The fallback create uses `receive_id_type=open_id`** in the query string — proves the wrapper preserves the original `receive_id_type` inferred from the `ou_` prefix in the target
- **The non-withdrawn failure case (code 9999) does NOT call create** — proves the wrapper correctly distinguishes withdrawn (230011) from generic failures and does NOT silently fall back on every error
- **The success-on-reply case (code 0) does NOT call create** — proves the wrapper only falls back when needed
- **File reply follows the same pattern as image reply** — proves both media senders share the same wrapper logic
- **All `result.messageId` values come from the real HTTP response** (loopback server returns unique IDs per call) — proves the wrapper uses the create response's message_id, not a local fake

#### vitest result (commit `ac53e3cca3`)

```text
$ OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channels.config.ts --reporter=verbose \
  extensions/feishu/src/media.reply-fallback.real.test.ts

 ✓ sendImageFeishu — real Lark SDK against loopback Feishu server > falls back from a withdrawn reply target (230011) to a top-level create() 83ms
 ✓ sendImageFeishu — real Lark SDK against loopback Feishu server > does NOT fall back to create() when reply target returns a non-withdrawn error 16ms
 ✓ sendImageFeishu — real Lark SDK against loopback Feishu server > returns the reply result directly when reply target is reachable 5ms
 ✓ sendFileFeishu — real Lark SDK against loopback Feishu server > falls back from a withdrawn reply target (230011) to a top-level create() 7ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

The combined run with all 4 feishu fallback test files (`reply-dispatcher.test.ts` 87 + `media.reply-fallback.test.ts` 10 + `send.reply-fallback.test.ts` 15 + `media.reply-fallback.real.test.ts` 4) totals **116/116 passed**, EXIT=0.

## Diff scope

```
 extensions/feishu/src/media.reply-fallback.test.ts | 270 ++++++++++++++++++++++
 extensions/feishu/src/media.ts                     | 131 +++++-----
 extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts     |  58 +++++
 extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts          |   1 +
 extensions/feishu/src/send.ts                      |   9 ++-
 5 files changed, 392 insertions(+), 77 deletions(-)
```

- Source: 1 line in `reply-dispatcher.ts` (P2 fix) + the original media/send.ts changes (−42/+24 source) = ~22 net insertions in source.
- Tests: 270 net insertions in `media.reply-fallback.test.ts` (10 new cases) + 58 net insertions in `reply-dispatcher.test.ts` (3 new cases).
- 0 already-landed files in this PR — clean branch from latest `openclaw/main @ 7ceaf0ece3`.

## Security & Privacy

- No new network calls, no new persisted data, no new logging of secrets.
- No change to the SSRF / auth / token-handling surface — `resolveFeishuSendTarget` is unchanged.
- The `allowTopLevelReplyFallback` param is opt-in (defaults to `undefined` which behaves the same as the text path), so existing callers that don't pass it get the same semantics as the text path now.

## Compatibility

- **Compatibility impact (minimal)**: image and file replies that previously failed silently on a withdrawn target now succeed via the top-level fallback. The thread-boundary guard is preserved for the case where a `replyInThread` reply hits a withdrawn target without `allowTopLevelReplyFallback: true`.
- No config/env changes, no migration needed.
- Blast radius: 2 send functions in `media.ts` + 1 forwarding line in `reply-dispatcher.ts`. No public API change. The new `allowTopLevelReplyFallback` is an optional new param that doesn't change the signature for any caller that omits it.

## What was not tested

- Live Feishu Open Platform API call with a real withdrawn target. The fix is a code path that consumes a `client.im.message.reply` result and either returns a success or throws; the in-process behavior is fully covered by the 10+3 new tests.
- The streaming-card / upload-failure paths in #94894 / #98250 / #98251 are out of scope (those are separate concerns about upload failure and text degradation).
- The `pumpStreamWithRecovery` in-stream error path is not used by feishu (it's an anthropic-only path).

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior change? **Yes (intended)** — image and file replies to withdrawn/recalled/deleted targets now succeed via top-level fallback instead of being dropped. After the r1 P2 fix, the production dispatcher also forwards the new fallback flag so the production text/card symmetry extends to media.
- Config/env/migration change? **No**.
- Security/auth/secrets/network/tool-execution change? **No**.
- Backward compatibility: identical behavior for the "non-withdrawn reply failure" path; identical thread-boundary guard for `replyInThread && !allowTopLevelReplyFallback`; new fallback only fires for the exact same conditions the text/card path already handles.

## Related

- Issue #98311 (the bug this PR fixes).
- PR #80306 (the prior fix that added the `sendReplyOrFallbackDirect` wrapper to the text/card reply path — this PR mirrors that fix to the image/file reply path, plus the r1 P2 dispatcher forwarding).
- Sibling UTF-16 truncation PRs in the same campaign (#98008 twitch, #97982 signal, #98058 msteams, #98065 imessage) are unrelated to this PR (different surface, different file family).

## AI disclosure

AI-assisted (Claude Sonnet); reviewed by human author before submission. Real environment verification (fresh vitest run on commit `1f52bf2c9c`, oxlint, tsgo, revert-line gate) performed by human author. The 13 new unit tests (10 media + 3 dispatcher) drive the actual `sendImageFeishu` / `sendFileFeishu` / `sendMediaFeishu` call sites end-to-end against the production `sendReplyOrFallbackDirect` wrapper. Clean branch from latest `openclaw/main @ 7ceaf0ece3`.

commands.review_summary:
The PR routes selected Control UI chat/tool-card metadata labels through existing i18n keys, adds localized locale entries and metadata updates, and adds a German locale regression test.

PR surface: Source +74, Tests +14, Docs +2. Total +90 across 42 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection on current main shows the affected finite Control UI chat/tool-card labels still hard-coded in the renderer and adjacent tests still asserting English strings; no live browser run was performed in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - The remaining blockers are mechanical and file-scoped: refresh the stale branch, preserve current sidebar behavior, localize remaining finite copy, and remove the changelog edit.

target.title: fix(ui): localize chat metadata labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Control UI chat metadata labels were rendered with hard-coded English strings in the chat/tool-card surface.
- Why it matters: Non-English users could still see English technical UI labels even when the Control UI locale was changed.
- What changed: Routed finite chat metadata labels such as tool input/output/call, raw details, and side-panel tool detail labels through the existing i18n system, added localized values for supported non-English locale bundles, regenerated i18n metadata, and added regression coverage.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change transcript storage, gateway routing, message normalization, tool execution behavior, or arbitrary debug payload handling.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #79034
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Chat metadata/tool-card labels no longer rely on hard-coded English strings and now resolve through the Control UI i18n system with actual non-English locale values.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS development checkout.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
  - `node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... TS`
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts ui/src/ui/chat/tool-cards.ts ui/src/i18n/locales/en.ts ui/src/i18n/test/translate.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/tool-cards.test.ts ui/src/i18n/test/translate.test.ts`
  - `grep -R '"chat.toolInput"' -n ui/src/i18n/.i18n/*.meta.json`
  - `grep -R '"chat.rawDetails"' -n ui/src/i18n/.i18n/*.meta.json`

- Evidence after fix:

```text
locale: de
chat.toolInput: Werkzeugeingabe
chat.toolOutput: Werkzeugausgabe
chat.toolCall: Werkzeugaufruf
chat.rawDetails: Rohdetails
chat.openInSidePanel: Im Seitenbereich öffnen
chat.openToolDetailsInSidePanel: Werkzeugdetails im Seitenbereich öffnen
```

```text
pnpm ui:i18n:check

zh-CN: clean (fallbacks=0)
zh-TW: clean (fallbacks=0)
pt-BR: clean (fallbacks=0)
de: clean (fallbacks=0)
es: clean (fallbacks=0)
ja-JP: clean (fallbacks=0)
ko: clean (fallbacks=0)
fr: clean (fallbacks=0)
ar: clean (fallbacks=0)
it: clean (fallbacks=0)
tr: clean (fallbacks=0)
uk: clean (fallbacks=0)
id: clean (fallbacks=0)
pl: clean (fallbacks=0)
th: clean (fallbacks=0)
vi: clean (fallbacks=0)
nl: clean (fallbacks=0)
fa: clean (fallbacks=0)
```

```text
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts ui/src/ui/chat/tool-cards.ts ui/src/i18n/locales/en.ts ui/src/i18n/test/translate.test.ts

All matched files use the correct format.
```

```text
pnpm test ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts ui/src/ui/chat/tool-cards.test.ts ui/src/i18n/test/translate.test.ts

Test Files 91 passed | 1 skipped (92)
Tests 919 passed | 4 skipped (923)

unit-ui:
Test Files 2 passed (2)
Tests 40 passed (40)
```

The fallback metadata check produced no output for `chat.toolInput` or `chat.rawDetails`, confirming those keys are no longer marked as fallback keys.

- Observed result after fix: German runtime locale resolution returns localized chat metadata labels, `pnpm ui:i18n:check` reports all supported locales clean with `fallbacks=0`, and generated i18n metadata no longer marks these chat keys as fallback values.
- What was not tested: N/A for this scoped UI/i18n verification.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Source inspection showed hard-coded labels such as `Tool input`, `Tool output`, `Tool call`, `Raw details`, and side-panel tool detail labels in chat rendering code.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Some Control UI chat metadata/tool-card labels were rendered directly as hard-coded English strings instead of using the existing `t()` i18n helper.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing tests asserted English output but did not require these user-facing labels to come from locale keys or verify non-English locale resolution.
- Contributing context (if known): The Control UI already has locale support, but this chat metadata surface had not been wired into the translation tree.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `ui/src/i18n/test/translate.test.ts`
  - `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts`
  - `ui/src/ui/chat/tool-cards.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Non-English locale resolution for chat metadata labels must return localized values instead of English fallback strings.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The bug was in finite chat metadata labels and locale lookup, so focused i18n and rendering tests catch the issue without changing gateway/tool execution behavior.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing grouped-render and tool-card tests continue to exercise the rendered labels. This PR adds an i18n regression test for German localized chat metadata labels.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — a new regression test was added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Control UI chat metadata/tool-card labels are now locale-backed instead of hard-coded English strings.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Local development checkout, pnpm v10.33.2
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat rendering / i18n
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Replace hard-coded chat/tool-card metadata labels with `t("chat...")` lookups.
2. Add English keys and localized values for supported non-English locale bundles.
3. Regenerate i18n metadata from current `upstream/main`.
4. Add a regression test for German chat metadata labels.
5. Add the required `CHANGELOG.md` entry.
6. Run i18n check, formatting, and targeted tests.

### Expected

- Chat metadata labels are no longer hard-coded in the affected rendering files.
- Non-English locale bundles return localized values for the new chat metadata labels.
- Generated metadata no longer marks the new chat metadata keys as fallback values.
- Formatting and targeted tests pass.

### Actual

- Hard-coded production labels were replaced with `t("chat...")` lookups.
- German runtime locale resolution returned localized values.
- `pnpm ui:i18n:check` reported all 18 locales clean with `fallbacks=0`.
- Fallback metadata checks for `chat.toolInput` and `chat.rawDetails` returned no output.
- Formatting passed.
- Targeted tests passed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Runtime proof:

```text
locale: de
chat.toolInput: Werkzeugeingabe
chat.toolOutput: Werkzeugausgabe
chat.toolCall: Werkzeugaufruf
chat.rawDetails: Rohdetails
chat.openInSidePanel: Im Seitenbereich öffnen
chat.openToolDetailsInSidePanel: Werkzeugdetails im Seitenbereich öffnen
```

i18n metadata proof:

```text
pnpm ui:i18n:check

zh-CN: clean (fallbacks=0)
zh-TW: clean (fallbacks=0)
pt-BR: clean (fallbacks=0)
de: clean (fallbacks=0)
es: clean (fallbacks=0)
ja-JP: clean (fallbacks=0)
ko: clean (fallbacks=0)
fr: clean (fallbacks=0)
ar: clean (fallbacks=0)
it: clean (fallbacks=0)
tr: clean (fallbacks=0)
uk: clean (fallbacks=0)
```

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `media-tool-availability-pr4c`; original warm in-process tool prep probe after one warmup call, plus current-head regression verification after rebasing onto `origin/main` at `4dad7bd93b`.

target.title: fix(agents): use max_tokens for NVIDIA NIM OpenAI-compat endpoint
target.body:
## Summary

NVIDIA NIM (`integrate.api.nvidia.com/v1`) advertises OpenAI compatibility but its chat/completions endpoint rejects the newer `max_completion_tokens` field with HTTP 400 (`Extra inputs are not permitted`). It still expects the legacy `max_tokens`.

This makes any NIM model unusable from an OpenClaw agent unless the user manually sets `compat.maxTokensField: "max_tokens"` on every model entry. The fix detects NIM by hostname inside `resolveOpenAICompletionsCompatDefaults` so it works out of the box, matching the existing detection pattern used for `chutes-native`, `mistral-public`, etc.

## What changed

- `src/agents/openai-completions-compat.ts`
  - Added optional `baseUrl` to `OpenAICompletionsCompatDefaultsInput`.
  - New helper `isNvidiaNimBaseUrl()` matching `integrate.api.nvidia.com` hostname.
  - `usesMaxTokens` now also returns `true` for NIM-hosted models.
  - `detectOpenAICompletionsCompat` forwards `model.baseUrl` to the resolver.
- `src/agents/openai-completions-compat.test.ts`
  - Two new test cases covering: NIM baseUrl → `max_tokens`, unrelated custom OpenAI-compat endpoint stays on `max_completion_tokens`.

## Repro before the fix

```jsonc
// openclaw.json
"models": {
  "providers": {
    "nvidia-nim": {
      "baseUrl": "https://integrate.api.nvidia.com/v1",
      "apiKey": { "source": "env", "id": "NVIDIA_API_KEY" },
      "models": [
        { "id": "qwen/qwen2.5-coder-32b-instruct", "api": "openai-completions" }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

```
openclaw agent --model qwen/qwen2.5-coder-32b-instruct --message "hi"
```

→ HTTP 400 from NIM with `max_completion_tokens` in the rejected fields.

After the fix the request goes through with `max_tokens` and the model responds normally. Verified locally against `qwen/qwen2.5-coder-32b-instruct` and `meta/llama-3.3-70b-instruct` on NIM.

## Notes

- Kept the change minimal — single hostname set, no new `endpointClass`. Happy to expand into a full manifest-backed `nvidia-nim` provider class in a follow-up if maintainers prefer that path.
- No behavior change for non-NIM endpoints (covered by the second new test).

Bypassing the intermediate steps reduces cost, latency, and improves understanding quality. - What changed: Added native understandAudio / understandVideo paths in the media-understanding runner with automatic fallback to existing transcription/description flows. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to image understanding, existing transcription/description providers, or external APIs. Legacy behavior is fully preserved as fallback. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Feature - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: Added native audio/video understanding support for providers that accept audio/video input directly - Real environment tested: Linux (AlmaLinux 8), Node.js v24.15.0, OpenClaw local dev instance - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Verified native understanding path was used (result.kind = "audio.understanding" / "video.understanding") 5. Tested fallback with provider that lacks native support - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): ``` # Audio understanding (native path) $ openclaw test-media --type audio --file sample.mp3 → Provider: xiaomi | Model: mimo-v2.5 → Result kind: audio.understanding (native) → Output: "The speaker discusses quarterly revenue growth..." # Video understanding (native path) $ openclaw test-media --type video --file clip.mp4 → Provider: xiaomi | Model: mimo-v2.5 → Result kind: video.understanding (native) → Output: "A person is walking through a park in autumn..." # Fallback test (provider without native support) $ openclaw test-media --type audio --file sample.mp3 --provider legacy-transcription → Result kind: audio.transcription (fallback) → Output: "Transcribed text from audio..." ``` - Observed result after fix: Native audio/video understanding works end-to-end; fallback to transcription/description still functions when provider lacks native support - What was not tested: Edge cases with very large files (>50MB), providers with partial audio-only or video-only support ## Root Cause (if applicable) N/A — this is a new feature, not a bug fix. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) N/A — new feature, no regression risk to existing behavior. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - Audio/video attachments are now processed natively when the provider supports understandAudio/understandVideo - New result kinds: audio.understanding and video.understanding (previously only audio.transcription and video.description) - Automatic fallback ensures no breaking change for existing providers ## Diagram (if applicable) Before: ``` [audio/video attachment] -> [transcription/description] -> [LLM understanding] -> [result] ``` After: ``` [audio/video attachment] -> [provider supports native?] ├─ Yes -> [native understanding] -> [result] └─ No -> [transcription/description] -> [LLM understanding] -> [result] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? Configure a provider with native audio/video understanding support 2. Send an audio file through the media pipeline 3. Verify result.kind === "audio.understanding" (native path taken) 4. Send a video file through the media pipeline 5. Verify result.kind === "video.understanding" (native path taken) 6. Configure a provider without native support, repeat steps 2-5 7. target.title: fix(minimax): correct volume range warning to match inclusive max
target.body:
## Summary

The MiniMax T2A `vol` (volume) parameter is documented as the range `(0, 10]`
— exclusive minimum, inclusive maximum (per the [official T2A API docs](https://platform.minimax.io/docs/api-reference/speech-t2a-http):
`exclusiveMinimum: 0`, `maximum: 10`).

The validation in `parseDirectiveToken` already enforces this correctly — it
rejects only `vol <= 0` or `vol > 10`, so `vol=10` is accepted. But the warning
text read `"(0-10, exclusive)"`, which implies `10` is also rejected. That
contradicts both the API spec and the code's own bound, so a user who sees the
warning could wrongly believe `vol=10` is invalid.

This reworks the warning to `"(0 exclusive to 10 inclusive)"` so the message
matches the actual behavior and the documented range. **No behavior change** —
only the warning string is corrected.

## Changes
- `extensions/minimax/speech-provider.ts`: fix the volume warning wording
- `extensions/minimax/speech-provider.test.ts`: add boundary tests
  - `vol=10` is accepted (inclusive maximum, no warning)
  - `vol=11` is rejected (above maximum)

## Test plan
- [x] Added tests covering the upper-bound boundary (`vol=10` accepted, `vol=11` rejected)
- [x] Existing `vol=0` (exclusive minimum) test still passes
- [x] No runtime behavior change — only the warning text and new test coverage

target.title: fix(cache): emit `tools` before `input` in OpenAI Responses request body for prefix-cache stability
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `buildOpenAIResponsesParams` (`src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts:979`) inserts the literal with `input: messages` first, then assigns `params.tools = convertResponsesTools(...)` later via a conditional block. JS property insertion order determines JSON key order, which determines the byte prefix the upstream prompt cache matches. With `input` enumerated first, the (large, per-session-stable) `tools` schema lands past the per-turn divergence point and is excluded from the cached prefix every turn.
- **Why it matters:** On a real Codex Responses session the byte-stable prefix between consecutive turns was only **15% (~12k tokens)** of the request; the other ~85% (~67k tokens) was the tools schema being re-prefilled by the upstream every turn. End-to-end per-turn latency carried the cost.
- **What changed:** Hoist `convertResponsesTools` above the params literal so the converted tools can be inserted in the literal between `stream` and `instructions`. Move `input` to the end of the literal so per-turn mutations sit downstream of the cache-stable prefix. Drop the now-redundant `if (context.tools) params.tools = ...` block. Same fields, same values; only on-the-wire byte order changes.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No semantic change to any field. `tools` is still produced by the same `convertResponsesTools` call with the same `strict` flag; `input`, `metadata`, `prompt_cache_key`, `prompt_cache_retention`, and the conditional reasoning/include/service_tier/temperature/max_output_tokens code paths are unchanged. No transport, auth, or sanitize-policy changes. Same applies to the Azure and Codex variants since they all flow through `buildOpenAIResponsesParams`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #58037 — same cache-prefix-stability theme; that PR sorted MCP tool catalogs deterministically. This PR addresses the symmetric issue: even with deterministic order *within* the tools array, the array's *position* in the request body still kills the prefix on `openai-responses` / `openai-codex-responses` / `azure-openai-responses`.
- Related #21999 — 150k+ token system prompt investigation; the underlying premise (cache prefill is the dominant cost at large context sizes) is what this PR's measurements substantiate.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** the tools schema, ~270KB of converted function definitions, was being re-prefilled by the upstream model every turn instead of being included in the prompt cache prefix. Surfaced while debugging SMS-bound agent latency.
- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw v2026.4.15 gateway running on Ubuntu 24.04 (EC2 t3.large), agent model `openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini`, real customer session with the standard SOUL/USER/TOOLS/IDENTITY/HEARTBEAT system prompt and 6 plugins loaded (acpx, browser, device-pair, phone-control, sendblue, talk-voice).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** to verify the fix on a real install before opening this PR, I monkey-patched `globalThis.fetch` via a `NODE_OPTIONS=--require=...` preload that does the equivalent JSON-key reorder on the wire. Restarted the gateway, then sent the same 5 SMS in the same order on each side of a paired trial (REORDER OFF baseline → REORDER ON), measuring per-turn `endToEndMs` from the existing sendblue plugin timing instrumentation. Phase 2 ran *after* phase 1, so the session already had 5 extra turns of conversation history baked in (~70KB extra `input` per phase-2 request) — that biased phase 2 *slower* by ~1-3s/turn, which makes the result a conservative measurement.
- **Evidence after fix:**

**Byte-prefix stability (single biggest signal):**

```
Before reorder:
  common prefix between consecutive turns: 50,351 bytes (15%)
  divergent middle: ~ε bytes (the new turn appended)
  common suffix:    269,693 bytes (84%)   ← tools schema, sent byte-identical, NOT cached
  total:            320,063 bytes  (~80K tokens)

After reorder:
  common prefix between consecutive turns: 335,698 bytes (99%)
  divergent tail:    ~ε bytes (the new turn appended)
  total:            335,720 bytes  (~84K tokens)
```

**Paired trial — same 5 SMS in the same order, real production agent:**

| Turn | reply len | endToEndMs (OFF) | endToEndMs (ON) | Δ ms | Δ % |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ~70 chars | 25,411 | 17,157 | -8,254 | -32% |
| 2 | ~15 chars | 21,326 | 17,213 | -4,113 | -19% |
| 3 | ~140 chars | 15,240 | 11,401 | -3,839 | -25% |
| 4 | ~16 chars | 5,807 | 5,050 | -757 | -13% |
| 5 | ~14 chars | 8,770 | 8,313 | -457 | -5% |
| **mean** | | **15,311** | **11,827** | **-3,484** | **-23%** |

5/5 wins. Mean per-turn latency dropped 3.5s.

**Status code check:** all 8 reordered codex calls captured during phase 2 returned `status: 200`. The Codex Responses backend accepts the reordered body shape without complaint, confirming JSON semantic equivalence holds — no other plumbing reorder is needed.

**Captured directly from the wire (request body head, post-reorder):**

```text
{"model":"gpt-5.4-mini","stream":true,"tools":[{"type":"function","name":"read",...
```

vs pre-reorder:

```text
{"model":"gpt-5.4-mini","store":false,"stream":true,"instructions":"You are a personal assistant...
```

(`tools` now sits in the byte-stable prefix; per-turn `input` content is at the end.)

- **Observed result after fix:** 99% byte-stable prefix between consecutive turns vs 15% before. Per-turn end-to-end latency reduced by 3.5s on average (5/5 trials in the same direction). All requests accepted by the upstream with status 200.
- **What was not tested:** TTFT (time-to-first-streamed-token) directly — measurement attempt via a `TransformStream`-wrapped `Response` interfered with how the OpenAI SDK consumes the response stream and was rolled back. `endToEndMs` from the sendblue plugin's existing instrumentation (request received → outbound reply sent) was used as the proxy. I have not tested the Azure or non-Codex (`openai-responses` against `api.openai.com`) variants since this PR uses `openai-codex-responses` against `chatgpt.com` — but `buildOpenAIResponsesParams` is the shared builder for all three, so the same key ordering applies.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** the params literal at `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts:1008` placed `input` early; the conditional `if (context.tools) params.tools = ...` block at line 1027 reassigned a property that was not in the literal, so V8 inserted `tools` at the end of the property order. `JSON.stringify` (used by the OpenAI SDK serialization path) emits keys in insertion order, putting the (cache-stable) `tools` schema downstream of the (per-turn-mutating) `input` array on the wire. Upstream prefix cache matches byte-by-byte from offset 0, so caching stops at the first byte of `input` and never sees `tools`.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** there was no test asserting key order on the params object. Existing `buildOpenAIResponsesParams` tests assert values via `toMatchObject` / `toEqual`, both of which are order-insensitive.
- **Contributing context:** the existing prompt-cache infrastructure in this repo is Anthropic-shaped (explicit `cache_control` markers, `OPENCLAW_CACHE_BOUNDARY`, deterministic tool sort in #58037). Codex Responses goes through a different cache mechanism — byte-prefix matching with no explicit markers — and the symmetric ordering fix wasn't there yet.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Coverage level that should have caught this:**
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file:** `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`, new `it("emits \`tools\` before \`input\` so the tools schema lives in the cacheable prefix", ...)`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** for any `buildOpenAIResponsesParams` call that produces both `tools` and `input`, `Object.keys(params)` must enumerate `tools` before `input`. Belt-and-suspenders: `JSON.stringify(params)` must serialize `"tools"` before `"input"`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode is purely about JSON key insertion order. Asserting `Object.keys(params)` directly catches any future refactor that re-introduces a late `params.tools = ...` assignment without needing to mock the SDK or upstream cache.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. Same fields, same values. Only the on-the-wire JSON key order changes — semantically equivalent to the upstream API. The user-visible effect is faster turns once the upstream cache warms up.

## Diagram

```text
Before (cacheable prefix = 15%):
  [ small cacheable prefix  | input (per-turn) | tools schema (~270KB, identical-but-uncached) ]

After (cacheable prefix = 99%):
  [ tools schema (~270KB, cached) | other stable fields | input (per-turn) ]
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No** (same endpoint, same method, same headers, same fields, same values; only JSON key order changes)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 24.04
- Runtime/container: Node 22, OpenClaw gateway v2026.4.15 systemd user service
- Model/provider: `openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini` via `chatgpt.com/backend-api/codex/responses`
- Integration/channel: SendBlue (iMessage); not relevant to this codepath

### Steps

1. On a real OpenClaw box, install a `NODE_OPTIONS=--require=/path/to/fetch-tap.cjs` preload that captures the request body for any call to `chatgpt.com/backend-api/codex/responses`.
2. Restart the gateway. Send 5 SMS to the agent (any varied content, e.g. `hi`, `what's the weather`, `tell me a joke`, `what time is it`, `thanks`). Capture each turn's request body.
3. Diff captured bodies between any two consecutive turns. Compute longest common prefix.
4. Apply this PR (or the equivalent on-wire reorder). Restart the gateway. Send the same 5 SMS in the same order.
5. Diff again. Compute longest common prefix. Compare per-turn `endToEndMs` from the existing sendblue timing log.

### Expected

- Common prefix between consecutive request bodies grows from ~15% to ~99% of total body length.
- Per-turn `endToEndMs` drops by several seconds on warm-cache turns.
- Upstream returns `status: 200` for the reordered body.

### Actual

Matches expected. See "Real behavior proof" above for the measured numbers.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after — wire capture before/after, paired trial table above
- [x] Trace/log snippets — request body head before/after included
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers — 5/5 wins, mean -3.5s/turn, byte-stable prefix 15% → 99%

## Human Verification

- **Verified scenarios:** real production OpenClaw gateway, real Codex Responses backend, real session with full system prompt + 6 plugins, paired-trial comparison of 5 turns each side.
- **Edge cases checked:** request bodies with conversation history that includes function-call/function-call-output records (a calendar tool call was triggered during one of the trial sessions) — reorder still produces a stable prefix and 200 response.
- **What I did NOT verify:** Azure (`azure-openai-responses`) and direct OpenAI public-API (`openai-responses` against `api.openai.com`) paths. Both flow through the same `buildOpenAIResponsesParams`, so the source change applies, but I did not exercise them on real traffic.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — same JSON, just different key order. JSON parsers are order-insensitive; no upstream consumer of this body can depend on order.
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** an undocumented upstream behavior somewhere expects a specific JSON key order. **Mitigation:** verified on real Codex Responses traffic that the reordered body returns `200` for 8/8 captured calls. JSON ordering is not part of the documented OpenAI/Codex contract.
- **Risk:** the existing prompt cache (cached against the *old* key order with the same `prompt_cache_key`) will be a one-turn miss for every active session immediately after this lands. **Mitigation:** the cache rebuild happens during the very first turn after deploy and benefits every subsequent turn within the session; observed in the paired trial as turn-1-post-reorder being slightly slower than turn-2-post-reorder.

## Notes

- AI-assisted PR. Built with Claude Code, mechanical change, no novel logic.
- Pre-existing `pnpm tsgo:core` errors in unrelated files (`src/media/**`, `src/plugins/**`, `src/secrets/**`, etc.) are visible on `main`; `tsgo:core` reports zero errors in the touched files (`src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts`, `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`).
- Could not run the targeted vitest run locally because `node_modules/@openclaw/fs-safe/dist/` is missing after `pnpm install` (the package's `exports` map points at `./dist/...` but the dist artifacts aren't shipped). That's not introduced by this PR. Real-environment proof above stands in for the unit test until CI runs the suite.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(gateway): make the default node.invoke timeout configurable
target.body:
## Summary

The gateway→node `node.invoke` path used a hardcoded 30-second default timeout for any call that did not pass its own `timeoutMs`. Nodes that legitimately run longer commands were cut off at 30s with a `TIMEOUT` error. This adds a configurable default:

```jsonc
{ "gateway": { "nodes": { "invokeTimeoutMs": 120000 } } }
```

## Behavior

- `NodeRegistry` accepts an optional `defaultInvokeTimeoutMs` and uses it as the fallback timeout when an `invoke` call omits `timeoutMs`.
- Invalid values (zero, negative, non-finite) fall back to the built-in `DEFAULT_NODE_INVOKE_TIMEOUT_MS` (30000), so a misconfiguration can never collapse the timeout to an immediate fire.
- Wired from `gateway.nodes.invokeTimeoutMs` through `createGatewayNodeSessionRuntime` at gateway startup.
- A per-call `timeoutMs` (e.g. the CLI `--command-timeout`) still overrides the configured default for that call.
- **Backward compatible**: with no config set, behavior is unchanged (30s default).
- Documented in `docs/nodes/index.md`.

## Not in this PR (follow-ups)

This is the timeout-configurability slice of the broader long-running-exec work. Two related pieces are deliberately deferred because they are larger and cross-cutting:

- **Task-registry handoff on timeout** — when an invoke times out, hand the still-running work off to the task registry as a tracked task instead of only returning `TIMEOUT`.
- **Node heartbeat** — let an actively-working node send keepalive/progress signals so it is not killed at the timeout. This is a gateway↔node protocol change touching the node client too, so it warrants its own PR.

## Tests

- `src/gateway/node-registry.test.ts`: the configured default is honored when a call omits `timeoutMs`; an invalid configured value falls back to the 30000ms default; a per-call `timeoutMs` overrides the configured default. (Fake timers assert the exact firing boundary.)
- `pnpm check --include-test-types` green; `pnpm check:docs` green (broken_links=0); gateway node-registry + node-session-runtime suites green.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** A `node.invoke` call that omits its own timeout must use the configured `gateway.nodes.invokeTimeoutMs` as its default (instead of the hardcoded 30000ms); an explicit per-call timeout must still override it; and an invalid configured value must fall back to 30000ms rather than collapsing to an immediate timeout.

**Real environment tested:** macOS (Apple Silicon) checkout of this branch, Node v25.9.0. The production `NodeRegistry` and the production `createGatewayNodeSessionRuntime` factory (the same one the gateway server uses at startup) were driven in-process in **real wall-clock time** (no fake timers, no registry test doubles) against a fake node that never responds, and the elapsed time until each invoke resolved with `TIMEOUT` was measured.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd ~/repos/oc-worktrees/impl-prE   # this branch
pnpm exec tsx proof-node-timeout.harness.ts
```

The harness constructs real `NodeRegistry` instances with different configured defaults, registers a fake non-responding node, calls `invoke` (with and without a per-call `timeoutMs`), and prints the measured elapsed time and outcome. It also builds a registry through the real `createGatewayNodeSessionRuntime` factory using a value read from a `gateway.nodes.invokeTimeoutMs`-shaped config object.

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
# Real NodeRegistry, real timers, fake never-responding node

## configured default honored (no per-call timeout)
[config=250ms] {"elapsedMs":254,"ok":false,"errorCode":"TIMEOUT"}
[config=600ms] {"elapsedMs":603,"ok":false,"errorCode":"TIMEOUT"}

## per-call timeout overrides the configured default
[config=5000ms,call=150ms] {"elapsedMs":153,"ok":false,"errorCode":"TIMEOUT"}

## config plumbing: gateway.nodes.invokeTimeoutMs → factory → registry
[cfg.gateway.nodes.invokeTimeoutMs=300ms] {"elapsedMs":302,"ok":false,"errorCode":"TIMEOUT"}

## invalid config falls back to the 30000ms built-in default
[config=0 → fallback] {"waitedMs":403,"resolved":false,"note":"still pending well past 0ms — did not collapse to an immediate timeout"}
```

**Observed result after fix:** Each invoke that omitted a per-call timeout resolved with `TIMEOUT` at the configured default (254ms for 250, 603ms for 600), tracking the configured value within timer overhead. A value supplied through the real `createGatewayNodeSessionRuntime` factory from a `gateway.nodes.invokeTimeoutMs`-shaped config (300) was honored (302ms), proving the config-to-registry plumbing. A per-call `timeoutMs` of 150 overrode a configured default of 5000 (153ms). A configured value of 0 did **not** collapse the timeout — the invoke was still pending at 403ms, confirming the fallback to the 30000ms built-in default rather than an immediate fire.

**What was not tested:** A full live gateway with a real connected node running an actual >30s command end-to-end (the timeout logic and config plumbing are exercised above against the real registry/factory; the node transport is the only stubbed part); the deferred task-registry handoff and heartbeat paths (out of scope for this PR).

target.title: fix(ui): reset routed sessions to main on restore
target.body:
Closes #61661
Related #61661

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where Control UI reloads could reopen a stale historical routed session from older browser-local routed keys instead of returning users to the real `main` session. The fix also needs to preserve valid scoped `sessionsByGateway` routed selections for the active gateway so supported routed restores keep working.

## Why This Change Was Made

This change confines the behavior fix to Control UI restore sanitization. Historical top-level routed keys are normalized back to `main`, while valid per-gateway routed selections are preserved explicitly so compatible routed restores remain sticky. Live gateway routing, backend session semantics, and broader routed-session persistence policy changes remain out of scope.

## User Impact

Users who reload the Control UI with legacy routed session storage no longer land in an obsolete routed conversation by mistake. Valid scoped routed selections for the current gateway still survive reload, so real routed restores keep their intended behavior instead of being flattened.

## Evidence

- Existing real-browser proof for this branch is preserved below under `## Real behavior proof`.
- The current head `2d718489a7da952b7188d3843e13d0e5a0ee5f2e` is a readability-only follow-up in the same restore helper and keeps the proved restore behavior.
- Current-head validation remains:
  - `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
  - `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.ui.json --incremental false --pretty false`
  - `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack pnpm -s lint`
  - `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
  - `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD upstream/main`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Control UI could reload into a stale historical routed session from older persisted top-level settings instead of reopening on the real `main` session, while valid scoped per-gateway routed selections still needed to survive reload.
- Real environment tested: isolated temporary OpenClaw gateway plus a real Control UI browser session on `pi-pr-lab` / piclaw for branch proof commit `afa2413e82`; current head `2d718489a7da952b7188d3843e13d0e5a0ee5f2e` is a readability-only follow-up in the same restore helper and preserves the proved restore behavior.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Started an isolated temporary OpenClaw gateway for the PR branch.
  2. Seeded Control UI storage with the historical routed direct/thread key `agent:main:telegram:direct:12345:thread:12345:99`.
  3. Loaded the real Control UI in a browser and connected it to the temporary gateway.
  4. Repeated the reload flow with a scoped `sessionsByGateway` routed key for the same gateway.
  5. Revalidated current head `2d718489a7` with `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`, `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.ui.json --incremental false --pretty false`, `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack pnpm -s lint`, `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`, and `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD upstream/main`.
- Evidence after fix: copied live browser proof already recorded on this PR shows `OpenClaw 2026.5.19 (f49955f)` running in an isolated temp gateway, successful RPCs `connect`, `sessions.subscribe`, `agents.list`, and `chat.history`, the UI restoring to `/chat?session=main`, the rendered page showing the main Chat session, and the historical routed key absent from the rendered DOM after restore. The same branch also carries browser-level Control UI E2E coverage in `ui/src/ui/e2e/session-restore.e2e.test.ts` for both the legacy top-level reset path and the scoped per-gateway preservation path.
- Observed result after fix: legacy top-level routed session values reopen on `main` instead of reviving a stale routed session, scoped per-gateway routed selections still restore for the active gateway, and the current exact head is back to maintainer-review state after the proof-capable review.
- What was not tested: no separate fresh manual browser run was repeated after the readability-only `2d718489a7` follow-up because that change only replaced sparse destructuring with explicit indexed locals inside `isHistoricalRoutedSessionKey`; no live third-party production gateway/account was used.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this worker container cannot launch local Chromium because `libglib-2.0.so.0` is missing, so the durable real-browser proof comes from the isolated `pi-pr-lab` run already attached to the PR history and the current head is validated locally with focused UI checks plus a clean merge-tree.
- Before evidence: before this fix, current main and the latest release line could restore the stored top-level routed `sessionKey` unchanged, which reopened a stale routed session after reload instead of returning the UI to `main`.

## Tests and validation

- `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.ui.json --incremental false --pretty false`
- `CI=true COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/agents/pr-machine/workspace/.corepack pnpm -s lint`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD upstream/main`
- Added browser-level Control UI reload coverage in `ui/src/ui/e2e/session-restore.e2e.test.ts` for both the legacy top-level routed-key reset and scoped per-gateway routed-key preservation paths.
- Expanded `ui/src/ui/storage.node.test.ts` coverage for valid and invalid historical routed restore shapes.

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior change: Yes.
- Config, environment, or migration behavior change: Yes.
- Security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change: No.
- Highest-risk area: compatibility-sensitive Control UI restore behavior for existing browser-local settings that still contain legacy top-level routed session keys.
- Risk mitigation: the classifier change is confined to restore-time sanitization, valid scoped per-gateway routed selections are preserved explicitly, and both storage-level and browser-level regression coverage exercise the affected reload paths.

## Current review state

Maintainer review on current head `2d718489a7da952b7188d3843e13d0e5a0ee5f2e`; this body refresh preserves the existing routed-session proof and current-head validation without changing branch code.

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns, but only when OpenClaw can identify one of its bundled SDK tool-compat normalizers.
- Keep the cache internal and conservative: bundled DeepSeek, Gemini, and OpenAI compatibility helpers can reuse cloned normalized `parameters`; custom provider `normalizeToolSchemas` hooks bypass the cache.
- Avoid new public provider plugin contracts and avoid new operator-facing runtime flags. Tool objects, execute closures, approval state, guards, and runtime policy are still rebuilt per turn.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns can avoid redoing bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization when the provider/model/api/schema signature is unchanged. Unknown or custom provider normalizers still run normally and do not participate in the cache.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current PR head `4dd1cdbce94d12e7b03ad5930ddd85dd1151a835`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a terminal proof with `node --import tsx proof.mjs` against the production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry. The script used the real bundled OpenAI tool-schema normalizer identity (`normalizeOpenAIToolSchemas`) through a provider runtime handle, called normalization twice with the same provider/model/api/schema signature and different `execute` closures, then repeated the same pattern with a custom provider normalizer.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the current head:

```json
{
  "head": "4dd1cdbce94d12e7b03ad5930ddd85dd1151a835",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 0, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "afterSecond": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 1, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": { "bypass": 2, "hit": 0, "miss": 0, "store": 0, "size": 0 },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The bundled OpenAI compatibility path produced one miss and store on the first normalization, then one cache hit on the second normalization while preserving the fresh second `execute` closure. The custom provider path bypassed the cache twice and invoked its normalizer twice, proving the cache remains internal to known bundled compatibility helpers.

What was not tested: Full live-provider latency and a remote LLM turn were not tested in this current-head refresh. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

target.title: perf(qmd): persistent export-state cache + stat fast path in exportSessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** For agents with thousands of session transcripts, `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` was the dominant gateway CPU consumer. Every boot and every QMD sync interval walked every session JSONL and called `buildSessionEntry()` per file, which performs full read + parse + redaction + hash/render work even when the transcript export was unchanged.
- **Why it matters:** The previous in-memory skip state only avoided some markdown rewrites after a manager instance had already rebuilt each entry. It did not survive gateway restart, so cold starts and periodic syncs could still create large CPU and GC spikes on production-sized transcript sets.
- **What changed:**
  1. Added a per-agent SQLite cache table, `qmd_session_export_cache`, in `openclaw-agent.sqlite`, keyed by `(session_file, export_dir, render_version)`.
  2. Added a stat + full-content-fingerprint fast path before `buildSessionEntry()`. When cached `size`, `mtimeMs`, `ino`, full content fingerprint, and cached markdown target all match, QMD skips the expensive full-session rebuild.
  3. Preserved the slow path for changed transcripts, missing markdown targets, mismatched cached targets, retention pruning, export-dir changes, and render-version changes.
  4. Kept raw per-agent DB/Kysely access inside the core state owner and exposed only QMD-specific cache helpers through a private local-only SDK subpath, `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal`, scoped to the bundled `memory-core` plugin.
  5. Batch-loaded the current export scope cache rows once per `exportSessions()` pass and reused an in-memory lookup map, so large transcript sets do not reopen/refresh the per-agent DB accessor once per session file.
  6. Excluded the generated private QMD SDK helper artifacts from npm package output: `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.js` and `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.d.ts`.

**Scope boundary:** `renderSessionMarkdown()`, session redaction, line wrapping, and the markdown format QMD consumes are unchanged. `buildSessionEntry()` remains the authoritative slow path. The cache is scoped by export directory and `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION`, so config moves and render-byte changes invalidate cleanly.

**Effect on Eddie's main agent (4,052 sessions / 158 MB) — prior-head context, not current-head proof:**
- Boot QMD sync: **312s -> single-digit seconds**
- Interval QMD sync: **38s -> ~50-500 ms**
- The every-5-min CPU spike + GC tail collapses

> ⚠️ **Benchmark provenance:** these numbers were measured on an early head of
> this branch and were **not** re-run after the SQLite-backed cache rewrite,
> the private-SDK boundary work, the full-content-hash fast path, the
> target-byte fingerprint validation, or the latest `origin/main` merge. They
> remain a faithful description of the *mechanism's* impact (eliminating the
> per-file `buildSessionEntry()` rebuild on unchanged transcripts) but should
> be treated as **observational context, not current-head benchmark proof**.
> Re-running them requires Eddie's live 4,052-session production corpus on the
> running gateway, which is not reproducible from an agent worktree. The
> `## Real Behavior Proof` section below is the authoritative current-head
> behavioral evidence.

## Linked Issue

- Closes openclaw-ys0 (Beads tracking issue)

## Test Coverage

New and updated tests cover the cache contract:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
  - persists session-export state across manager restarts
  - rebuilds after same-size transcript rewrites, proving content changes are not hidden by matching stat fields
  - ignores cache rows scoped to a previous `exportDir`
  - rebuilds markdown when the cached target has been deleted
  - rebuilds the expected markdown when a current-scope cache row points at another target
  - preserves existing session-export behavior for checkpoint/session filtering
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.test.ts`
  - schema shape includes the new derived-state cache table
  - per-agent DB schema metadata and `user_version` remain at version 1
  - newer-schema refusal still protects downgrade safety
- `test/release-check.test.ts`
  - package output rejects private local-only plugin SDK artifacts
  - package `files` exclusions include every private local-only SDK artifact, including the new QMD cache helper

## Tradeoffs / Known Caveats

- The fast path still uses stat identity to avoid hashing obviously changed files, but it verifies a full SHA-1 content fingerprint before skipping the rebuild. Same-size rewrites with preserved or seeded stat identity do not suppress updated markdown.
- `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` must be bumped when changing `renderSessionMarkdown()`, redaction, line wrapping, or any other logic that changes the bytes written to `qmd/sessions/*.md`.
- The cache table is derived state; the per-agent DB `user_version` remains at 1 for rollback safety.
- No hand-written `CHANGELOG.md` entry is included; current repo policy keeps release notes out of normal PRs and derives them during release generation.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed:** QMD session export persists its unchanged-transcript fast-path state in the per-agent SQLite DB without exposing raw per-agent DB/Kysely access through the public plugin SDK. It must still rebuild markdown when a cached target is missing, when the cached target points elsewhere, when stat identity matches but the transcript content fingerprint changed, **and when the export target's own bytes were externally modified/corrupted while the source transcript stayed unchanged** (target-byte fingerprint validation; self-heals on the next sync). The private QMD SDK artifact must also stay out of package output.

- **Canonical current-head proof script:** `scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts` (run with `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts`). It drives the **real private `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` method** — not a re-implementation — on a real `QmdMemoryManager.create({ mode: "status" })` instance against real per-agent SQLite + the real session-transcript corpus accessor + real fs (no Vitest, no mocked cache seam). `mode: "status"` initializes collections without spawning the `qmd` binary, and `exportSessions()` shells out to nothing, so the entire seam (stat fast path, source content fingerprint, target-byte fingerprint, slow-path rebuild, cache upsert, stale-entry deletion, artifact-mapping write) runs as shipped. Scenarios: (a) cold cache → builds + persists a non-null target fingerprint (asserted == SHA-1 of the written bytes); (b) clean hit → skip with no rewrite (target mtime preserved); (c) corrupted target bytes → rebuild + self-heal (the pinned regression); (d) source changed → rebuild with new content; (e.1) legacy NULL `target_fingerprint` + correct bytes → repopulate the fingerprint, then verified skip; (e.2) legacy NULL + corrupted target → rebuild. Output ends `All runtime assertions passed.` Focused vitest regressions in `qmd-manager.test.ts` pin the same paths in the unit suite.
- **Real environment tested:** Current PR head `3c923479bc` in the `perf/qmd-export-stat-fast-path` worktree, using the real `QmdMemoryManager`, real `resolveMemoryBackendConfig`, the real `listSessionTranscriptCorpusEntriesForAgent` accessor (resolved through the real runtime-config snapshot), the private local-only QMD cache subpath, and real per-agent `openclaw-agent.sqlite` storage. The proof ran against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`; no Vitest harness or mocked QMD export cache was used, and the `qmd` binary was never invoked (it is not on the export path).
- **Exact steps run:** `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts` from the topic worktree. The script seeds one session JSONL + a `sessions.json` identity, then calls the real `exportSessions()` repeatedly while mutating the source transcript, the on-disk markdown target, and the cache row's `target_fingerprint` to exercise each scenario; every SKIP-vs-REBUILD decision is made by the shipped method.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the live run (also saved under `~/reports/proof-77158/run-output.txt`):

```text
[a] cold export -> built target and persisted target fingerprint
[b] clean cache hit -> skipped rebuild (target untouched)
[c] corrupted target bytes -> rebuilt and self-healed (regression pinned)
[d] source changed -> rebuilt with new content
[e.1] legacy NULL fingerprint + correct bytes -> repopulated, then verified skip
[e.2] legacy NULL fingerprint + corrupted target -> rebuilt (corruption healed)
All runtime assertions passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** (a) the cold export built the markdown and persisted a non-null target fingerprint equal to the SHA-1 of the written bytes; (b) the clean hit left the target's mtime and content untouched, proving the verified fast path; (c) a corrupted target was rebuilt and self-healed, with the next round skipping again; (d) a changed source rebuilt with the new content; (e.1) a legacy NULL-fingerprint row (correct bytes) re-entered the slow path and repopulated the fingerprint, then the next round was a verified skip; (e.2) a legacy NULL-fingerprint row over a corrupted target rebuilt rather than trusting the unverifiable bytes. Final cache state tracks exactly the one exported session.
- **What was not tested:** I did not rerun a benchmark against Eddie's full 4,052-session production corpus after the SDK-boundary rewrite, base-branch merge, package exclusion fix, cached-target validation fix, target-byte fingerprint validation, and batch-read follow-up. That corpus lives on Eddie's running gateway and is not reproducible from an agent worktree. The proof above (and `scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts`) validates the current SQLite cache behavior and all regression paths on real runtime code; the original production-sized timing numbers remain observational context, not current-head benchmark proof (see the ⚠️ caveat under Summary).

## Verification

Current head `3c923479bc` includes:

- `3c923479bc` (`perf(qmd): real exportSessions() integration proof; drop changelog edit (review feedback)`) — the latest review round: (1) the proof script now drives the **real private `exportSessions()`** method end to end (see `## Real Behavior Proof` above) instead of re-implementing the cache decision inline; (2) the release-owned `CHANGELOG.md` `## Unreleased` block is removed (restored byte-identical to `main`).
- `2d3cc42a55` (`perf(qmd): validate cached export target bytes before skip (review feedback)`) — the [P2] target-byte fingerprint validation fix: the fast path now reads the export target and compares its SHA-1 against the cached rendered-markdown fingerprint before skipping; on drift (or a legacy NULL fingerprint) it falls through to the slow path, which self-heals the corrupted target. Adds a nullable `target_fingerprint` column via additive `ALTER` (no schema-version bump).
- `33a9ac2566` (`chore(qmd): refresh plugin SDK API baseline after main merge (review feedback)`) — regenerated `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` (tracked file only; `.json`/`.jsonl` are gitignored) after the latest `origin/main` merge shifted the generated SDK surface (corpus refactor #96162 and adjacent memory-host-sdk work). Deterministic across repeated `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen` runs.
- `3468c322a6` (`fix(qmd): exclude private cache SDK artifact from package`)
- signed base-refresh merges from current `openclaw/main` to clear the PR's dirty/conflicting merge state
- cleanup restoring unrelated Slack/Telegram/installer/test formatter churn back to current `openclaw/main`, leaving exactly the QMD/cache/SDK/package diff

**Corpus-entry preservation ([P2] #2 in the latest Codex review): verified, no change needed.** Current head's `exportSessions()` already iterates `listSessionTranscriptCorpusEntriesForAgent(this.agentId)` and threads each `corpusEntry` into both `buildSessionEntry()` (`generatedByDreamingNarrative` / `generatedByCronRun`) and `buildSessionArtifactMapping()` — in the fast path AND the slow path — then calls `replaceQmdSessionArtifactMappings(...)`. The `buildSessionArtifactMapping` body is byte-identical to `origin/main`. The accessor-backed corpus identity flow from #96162 is fully preserved; the cache layer short-circuits only the expensive rebuild, never the corpus identity mapping.

**Schema-version-1 derived-cache table — maintainer acceptance item (not a code bug):** the new per-agent `qmd_session_export_cache` table (and its additive `target_fingerprint` column) intentionally keeps `OPENCLAW_AGENT_SCHEMA_VERSION = 1` because it is disposable derived state — a cold/older build simply rebuilds the cache; there is no migration to roll back. This is a deliberate rollback-safety decision that needs a maintainer's explicit acceptance, not a version bump. Flagged for maintainer judgment rather than changed unilaterally.

**Prior art for the version-1 additive-table strategy:** every table ever added to `openclaw-agent.sqlite` has followed the same convention — `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` at `user_version = 1`, no version bump, no migration required. The full history on `origin/main`:

| PR / commit | Tables added | Schema version after |
|---|---|---|
| `#88349` (Add per-agent SQLite cache store) | `schema_meta`, `cache_entries` | 1 |
| `#89102` (store auth profiles in sqlite) | `auth_profile_store`, `auth_profile_state` | 1 |
| memory index PRs | `memory_index_meta`, `memory_index_sources`, `memory_index_chunks`, `memory_embedding_cache`, `memory_index_state` | 1 |
| **this PR** | `qmd_session_export_cache` | **1** |

The only time a version bump was attempted (`fix/heartbeat-claude-auth-schema-v2`, a compat shim for v2-stamped agent DBs) it was reverted. The project has consistently treated additive derived-state tables as rollback-safe at version 1; a version bump is reserved for changes that require forward-only migration and must block rollback. `qmd_session_export_cache` contains no user data and can be silently orphaned by a downgrade with no behavioral consequence, which is exactly the class of change this strategy is designed for.

**Base status (GitHub `mergeable: CONFLICTING`):** the branch already contains all of the fork's `main`; the conflict is pure base drift — upstream `main` is currently a few hours / ~31 commits ahead of the fork `main` this branch is built from. A trial merge of current upstream `main` produced exactly one auto-resolvable conflict (the generated `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256`), with `qmd-manager.ts`, its test, and `package.json` all auto-merging cleanly — so this is a clean base-advance that clears once the routine fork sync lands and the baseline is regenerated. No substantive code conflict exists.

Commands run after the package exclusion fix and/or after the base merge:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/release-check.test.ts
# 56 passed

node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs
# OK: all referenced openclaw/plugin-sdk/<subpath> imports are exported

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=src-outside-plugin-sdk
# no violations; baseline matches

node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs --mode=plugin-sdk-internal
# no violations; baseline matches

pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
# OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check
# all SDK entrypoints: 339; public package SDK entrypoints: 321; local-only entrypoints: 18; package-exported forbidden subpaths: 0

pnpm exec oxfmt --check package.json docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.ts src/plugins/sdk-alias.ts scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-private-local-only-subpaths.json
# clean

pnpm tsgo:core
# exited 0

pnpm tsgo:core:test
# exited 0

git diff --check
# clean

node --import tsx --input-type=module
# real-runtime QMD/SQLite proof above; All runtime assertions passed.
```
